coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of gadolinium (III) chelates functionalized carbon quantum dots for fluorescence and magnetic resonance dual-modal bioimaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Yupeng
Pan, Yi
Zhong, Jian
Yang, Jun
Zheng, Junhui
Cheng, Jingliang
Song, Rong
Yi, Changqing
coredata.dc:description: Multi-modal imaging probes through synergistically combining two or more imaging modalities into single nanoparticle, offer possibilities to address multiple issues such as resolution, sensitivity, and tissue penetration. In this study, we report a facile strategy to synthesize a dual-modal fluorescence/magnetic resonance (MR) imaging nanoprobe with bright fluorescence and significantly improved relaxivity, where Gd3+ ions are simply chelated onto the outer surface of the cyclic DTPA dianhydride (cDTPAA) functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and zeta potential measurements validated the successful preparation of CQD–DTPA–Gd. The surface conjugation of CQDs with cDTPAA and further Gd3+ chelation does not perturb core optical properties of CQDs, and the thus prepared CQD–DTPA–Gd contain minimum Gd3+ content (8.06%, w/w), while exhibiting significantly improved longitudinal relaxivity (56.72mM−1 s−1). Excellent water solubility, good cell-membrane permeability and negligible cytotoxicity make CQD–DTPA–Gd an ideal dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging probe, suggesting its potential and significance in practical bioimaging applications and medical translation in the future.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis routes for the CQD-based dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging nanoprobe. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(A) HRTEM image of CQD–DTPA–Gd. (B) HRTEM image of single nanoparticle with the lattice spacing of CQD in CQD–DTPA–Gd. Inset: histograms of particles size distribution of CQD–DTPA–Gd.

(A) FTIR spectra of (a) the as-prepared CQDs, (b) CQD–DTPA, (c) CQD–DTPA–Gd. XPS survey spectra (B), Gd 4d spectra (C) and Gd 3d (D) spectra of (a) the as-prepared CQDs, (b) CQD–DTPA, (c) CQD–DTPA–Gd. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (a), excitation spectrum (b), wavelength-dependent emission spectra (c) of CQD–DTPA–Gd. The inset is the photograph of the CQD–DTPA–Gd under bright and 365nm UV light. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) T 1-weighted MR images of CQD–DTPA–Gd with various concentrations (equivalent Gd concentration: 0, 0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.00mM). (B) Linear correlation between longitudinal relaxivities (r 1) and equivalent Gd concentration of CQD–DTPA–Gd nanoprobe. The value of T 1 −1 was collected basing on readings from (A).

The confocal fluorescent microscopic images of HeLa cells treated with 0.2mgmL−1 CQD–DTPA–Gd for 4h. Images were taken under (A) bright field, (B) 405nm diode laser excitation, (C) 458nm Ar-laser excitation, and (D) 514nm Ar-laser excitation. (E–G) overlay of fluorescence and bright field images. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of gadolinium (III) chelates functionalized carbon quantum dots for fluorescence and magnetic resonance dual-modal bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Sensing Technology and Biomedical Instruments (Guangdong Province), School of Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Sensing Technology and Biomedical Instruments (Guangdong Province)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of MRI, The First Affiliated Hospital of Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Affiliated Hospital of Zhengzhou University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-modal imaging probes through synergistically combining two or more imaging modalities into single nanoparticle, offer possibilities to address multiple issues such as resolution, sensitivity, and tissue penetration. In this study, we report a facile strategy to synthesize a dual-modal fluorescence/magnetic resonance (MR) imaging nanoprobe with bright fluorescence and significantly improved relaxivity, where Gd3+ ions are simply chelated onto the outer surface of the cyclic DTPA dianhydride (cDTPAA) functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and zeta potential measurements validated the successful preparation of CQD–DTPA–Gd. The surface conjugation of CQDs with cDTPAA and further Gd3+ chelation does not perturb core optical properties of CQDs, and the thus prepared CQD–DTPA–Gd contain minimum Gd3+ content (8.06%, w/w), while exhibiting significantly improved longitudinal relaxivity (56.72mM−1 s−1). Excellent water solubility, good cell-membrane permeability and negligible cytotoxicity make CQD–DTPA–Gd an ideal dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging probe, suggesting its potential and significance in practical bioimaging applications and medical translation in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Noninvasive molecular imaging is now a powerful tool for early diagnosis and fundamental research of malignant tumors, because it enables the visualization of cellular function and molecular process in living organisms without perturbing them. Various modalities such as magnetic resonance (MR), ultrasound, fluorescence, X-ray computed tomography (CT), positron emission tomography, and single photon emission CT, can be used for noninvasive molecular imaging [1–6]. Depending on the imaging principle for deriving the visual information, every individual modality has its own merit and deficiency, resulting in difficulties in quick and accurate data collection and disease diagnosis relying on single imaging mode. For example, MR imaging modality reveals the merits of non-ionizing, high spatial resolution, and deep tissue penetration, but suffers the shortcomings of relatively poor sensitivity. However, fluorescence imaging has the capacity for single-cell sensitivity and subcellular resolution, but possesses poor spatial resolution and tissue penetration.

Therefore, it is highly desirable to design novel imaging agents which are synergistically combining two or more imaging modalities to address issues such as resolution, sensitivity, and tissue penetration [7–16]. While, nanoparticles (NPs) provide a robust framework in which two or more components can be integrated to offer multifunctional and synergetic applications [17,18]. In this context, nanoscale dual-modal imaging probes combining fluorescence/MR modalities offer possibilities to obtain multiple imaging data for more precise cancer diagnosis and its more efficient management [10–16]. Fluorescent NPs such as organic dye or metal complex-encapsulated silica NPs (SiNPs) [10,11], semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [12], lanthanide complexes and upconversion NPs [13–15], have been employed as the framework in the construction of dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging probes, where covalently or non-covalently conjugated iron oxide nanoparticles and/or gadolinium chelates always serve as the magnetic probes.

Current trend in the development of dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging probes suggests that an ideal nanoprobe should not only own stronger fluorescent intensity and higher relaxivity, but also should possess low toxicity and good stability in a biological environment. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is becoming a superior framework to construct multi-modal imaging probes, because of their advantages such as green synthesis, the ease of surface functionalization, favorable optical properties, and excellent biocompatibility [19–27]. Bourlinos et al. separately use tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane as a carbon source and gadopentetic acid as a Gd(III) source to synthesize Gd-doped CQDs for fluorescence/MR imaging [19]. In the report of Ren et al., gadopentetate monomeglumine was employed as both the carbon source and Gd(III) source to prepare Gd-doped CQDs [20]. One-pot approach such as hydrothermal treatment of citrate acid (CA), ethanediamine, and GdCl3 at 200°C [21], or microwave-assisted polyol method using sucrose, concentrated H2SO4, and GdCl3 [22], have also been successfully demonstrated to prepare Gd-doped CQDs. Recently, Gd-doped CQDs are also prepared by simple calcination of gadopentetic acid (Gd–DTPA) [23]. Nevertheless, exploration of CQDs as multi-modal imaging nanoprobes for biological processes monitoring still remains in the early stage.

Considering the diversity in the preparation, surface functionalization, and photoluminescent property of CQDs, continued efforts should be exerted. Although one-pot pyrolysis approach using different precursors offer a straightforward way to prepare dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging nanoprobes [19–23], longitudinal relaxivities (r 1) of Gd-doped CQDs should be further improved for their practical applications in bioimaging and future medical translation. It has been reported that longitudinal relaxivity largely depends on direct interactions between Gd3+ and hydrogen protons, suggesting that only outer surface-chelated Gd3+ of Gd-doped CQDs could contribute to longitudinal relaxivity [22,28]. And it is well-known that Gd3+ is highly toxic and there is no biochemistry based on Gd3+ in human body [29]. Therefore, decreasing total amount of Gd3+ in Gd-doped CQDs while increasing their surface-chelated Gd3+ would favor their practical applications in bioimaging.

In this report, a dual-modal imaging probe with bright fluorescence and improved relaxivity for T 1-weighted MR imaging was prepared and investigated in detail. As showed in Fig. 1 , CQDs were firstly prepared as the framework for the dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging nanoprobe by the low temperature pyrolysis method with CA monohydrate as the carbon source and branched polyethyl-enimine (PEI) as the co-reactant [30,31]. Then, cyclic DTPA dianhydride (cDTPAA) is covalently conjugated onto the outer surface of CQDs through carbodiimide chemistry to produce CQD–DTPA, which can further complex with the consistent mole of Gd3+ [32,33]. The thus prepared CQD–DTPA–Gd exhibits excellent water solubility and cell-membrane permeability, which provides opportunities for dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging in vitro and in vivo. Experimental results show that CQD–DTPA–Gd prepared in this study contain minimum Gd3+ content, while exhibiting significantly improved longitudinal relaxivity and negligible cytotoxicity, since Gd3+ ions are only chelated onto the outer surface of the nanoprobes. The CQD–DTPA–Gd has also been demonstrated for dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging of HeLa cells in real-time, suggesting their potential and significance in practical bioimaging applications and medical translation in the future.

Morphology characterization of the nanoparticles was performed with a JEOL JEM-1400 transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements, UV–Vis and fluorescent spectra were recorded using a VERTEX 70 spectrometer (Bruker), an ESCALab250 spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher), a NanoZS90 instrument (Malvern), a Beckman DU730 UV–Vis spectrometer and a Fluoromax-4P spectrometer (Horiba), respectively.

The amine-terminated CQDs were synthesized by low temperature pyrolysis of CA in the presence of PEI [30,31,34]. In brief, 0.5g PEI and 1.0g CA were dissolved uniformly with 10mL boiling water in a 25mL beaker, and then heated moderately (∼180°C) by oil bath heating. The mixture was heated to evaporate most water to obtain a uniform pale-yellow gel, followed by the addition of 1mL cold water under heating. The same procedure was repeated for 10 times, until the color of gel turned from yellow to orange, indicating the formation of amine-terminated CQDs. Then, the as-prepared CQDs were purified by silica gel column chromatography, as described in detail elsewhere [30,31,34].

cDTPAA was prepared according to the protocol of Paik [32]. In brief, DTPA (7.9g, 0.02mol), acetic anhydride (8mL, 0.085mol) and pyridine (12mL, 0.15mol) were added to a 50mL round bottom flask and the solution was stirred under reflux at 65°C for 24h. The products were filtered, washed thoroughly with acetic anhydride and ether, and finally freeze-dried.

Well-established protocol of carbodiimide chemistry was employed for the surface functionalization of CQDs with DTPA. Typically, 30mg cDTPAA and 2.6×10−2 mmol EDC were slowly added to 2mL of 5.0mgmL−1 amine-terminated CQDs suspension (pH 7.4 in PBS) under stirring at room temperature for 3h to obtain CQD–DTPA. Then, 0.10mol GdCl3 was slowly added under stirring to synthesize CQD–DTPA–Gd. The pure product was obtained by 2days’ dialysis against de-ionized (D.I) water to remove the small molecules using a tubular membrane of cellulose (MWCO 1.0kDa). The residual suspension was finally freeze-dried to give CQD–DTPA–Gd.

Longitudinal relaxation times (T 1) of serial dilutions of CQD–DTPA–Gd were measured on a clinical 3.0T MRI instrument, where a standard inversion recovery pulse sequence was applied and water protons in D.I water was used as the blank. A 1.5 T human clinical scanner applied with a standard spinecho sequence, 500ms TR and 12ms TE was employed to collect T 1-weighted MR images of serial dilutions of CQD–DTPA–Gd.

The HeLa cells (supplied by Cells Bank of the Chinese Academy of Science) were cultured in H-DMEM (high glucose) supplemented with 10% FBS, penicillin (50UmL−1), and streptomycin (0.05mgmL−1) in an atmosphere of 5% CO2/95% air at 37°C. Cytotoxicity of CQD–DTPA–Gd was evaluated by testing the mitochondrial enzyme function according to the colorimetric 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) method using 5.0μM anti-cancer drug cisplatin as positive control. In brief, CQD–DTPA–Gd with Gd3+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 1.0mM was added to HeLa cells, co-incubated with HeLa cells for 24h, followed by washed twice with PBS. Then, 10% MTT agent in 500μL culture medium was added, and incubated for another 4h, followed by removing the supernatant. The formazan crystals were solubilized by adding 100μL DMSO and shaken for 10min, followed by colorimetric analysis with a microplate reader. Each experiment was performed in triplicate.

For in vivo cell imaging assay, HeLa cells were seeded on 6mm glass coverslips (1×105 cells per slip) and allowed to adhere for 24h, followed by the treatment with 0.2mgmL−1 CQD–DTPA–Gd suspension for 4h. Then, the cells were washed several times with PBS (pH=7.4), and were imaged using a confocal laser scanning microscope (LSM 710, Carl Zeiss). Particularly, in order to demonstrate the characteristic wavelength-dependent emission of CQDs, the cells were respectively illuminated with 405nm diode laser, 458nm and 514nm Ar-laser. The pinhole and gain settings of the detector were kept constant.

Since carboxyl groups are able to facilitate the dehydration and carbonization, CA is frequently used as carbon source to prepare CQDs through pyrolysis methods [30,31,34–37]. It has been reported that the addition of surface-passivation reagents especially amine molecules during the pyrolysis would significantly increase the quantum yield of the resultant CQDs [38]. And PEI is a water-soluble polymer with extremely high density of ethylene diamine units for further chemical modification [30,31,34]. Therefore, in this study, the low temperature pyrolysis method with CA monohydrate as the carbon source and PEI as the co-reactant was employed to prepare CQDs with the abundant surface primary amine. A typical TEM image of PEI-passivated CQDs was shown in Fig. S1A, where the as-prepared CQDs exhibited a round-like geometry with a diameter of ∼5.0nm, and were highly dispersible in water. DLS measurements revealed that as-prepared CQDs exhibited with a narrow size distribution between 4.0 and 8.0nm, which was well correlated with high resolution TEM (HRTEM) measurements (Fig. S1B). Kaiser tests have been performed to determine the free amines on the surface of as-prepared CQDs, and results in Fig. S2 confirmed that there are 8.81% (w/w) free amines groups. The surface conjugation of CQDs with cDTPAA and further Gd3+ chelation did not obviously affect the morphology of CQDs, as evidenced by HRTEM images (Fig. 2 A). CQD–DTPA–Gd were spherical in shape with obvious lattice spacing of 0.213nm which is consistent with that of (100) facet of graphite (Fig. 2B) [34], suggesting the surface functionalization did not change the crystal structure of CQDs. This result is quite important because the preserve of the original crystal structure suggests that core optical properties of CQDs would not be perturbed by the synthesis procedures. Again, DLS measurements correlated well with HRTEM images, where CQD–DTPA–Gd showed a size distribution between 4.0 and 6.0nm (inset of Fig. 2A).

Benefitting from the abundant amine groups on the as-prepared CQDs, cDTPAA is covalently conjugated onto the outer surface of CQDs through carbodiimide chemistry to produce CQD–DTPA, which further complex with the consistent mole of Gd3+ to prepare CQD–DTPA–Gd [32,33]. FTIR, zeta potential measurements, and XPS were employed to monitor the reaction processes through characterizing samples step-by-step. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the FTIR spectra of the as-prepared CQDs presented the vibration band of amine groups at 3440cm−1 (νN–H) and 1587.4cm−1 (δN–H), validating the presence of free –NH2 groups on their surface for further functionalization (curve a of Fig. 3A). Compared to the as-prepared CQDs, the absorption peak of νCO with a slight shift from 1712.7cm−1 to 1627.9cm−1 and significantly enhanced intensity, was observed in the FTIR spectra of CQD–DTPA (curve b of Fig. 3A). This result is quite important because the vibration peak νCO is expected to increase significantly after the covalent conjugation of cDTPAA, thanks to the contribution from the newly formed amide bonds between cDTPAA and CQDs, and the carboxylic groups of cDTPAA hydrolyzate. Similarly, the vibration peak νC–N at ∼1390cm−1 with significantly enhanced absorption intensity was also observed, suggesting the successful conjugation of cDTPAA onto the surface of CQDs. After chelation with Gd3+, no obvious change in peak shape was observed, except that the vibration peaks of νCO and νC–N exhibited a slight shift to 1593.1cm−1 and 1406.1cm−1 (curve c of Fig. 3A), respectively.

The surface conjugation of CQDs with cDTPAA and further Gd3+ chelation would change the surface charges of CQDs after each reaction step. The introduction of cDTPAA onto the surface of CQDs through carboxylation of the amine groups is expected to decrease their surface charges, while further Gd3+ chelation should increase their surface charges. The change of zeta potential of CQDs (17.1mV), CQD–DTPA (6.08mV) and CQD–DTPA–Gd (20.8mV) could well take account of the above facts, providing a strong evidence for the successful functionalization of CQDs.

The successful formation of CQD–DTPA–Gd was further verified by XPS spectra. XPS survey spectra confirmed the as-prepared CQDs (curve a of Fig. 3B) and CQD–DTPA (curve b of Fig. 3B) were mainly composed of C, N, and O, showing the characteristic peaks corresponding to C 1s (285.6eV, Fig. S3A), N 1s (399.32eV, Fig. S3B), O 1s (531.9eV, Fig. S3C). As expected, XPS results confirmed the presence of Gd in the product with 49.98% C, 27.23% O, 11.97% N, and 7.07% Gd (Table S1). CQD–DTPA–Gd showed additional characteristic peaks corresponding to Gd 4d (142.9eV) and Gd 3d (1188.0eV), confirming the successful Gd3+ chelation onto the surface of CQD–DTPA (curve c of Fig. 3B). The high-resolution XPS 4d spectra of CQD–DTPA–Gd (curve c of Fig. 3C) exhibited peaks at 142.9 and 148.1eV, while no such peaks could be detected for the as-prepared CQDs (curve a of Fig. 3C) and CQD–DTPA (curve b of Fig. 3C). Fig. 3D showed the high-resolution XPS Gd 3d spectra of CQDs (curve a of Fig. 3D), CQD–DTPA (curve b of Fig. 3D), and CQD–DTPA–Gd (curve c of Fig. 3D). The Gd 3d spectra of CQDs and CQD–DTPA showed no signal above the detection limit of the instrument, even with extensive signal averaging. Again, CQD–DTPA–Gd exhibited Gd 3d5/2 line at 1188.0eV, strongly suggesting the successful Gd3+ chelation onto the surface of CQDs. The different coordination environments possibly result in the higher binding energy in comparison to the corresponding elemental Gd 3d5/2 (1186.0eV) [22,39]. The presence of both Gd 4d and Gd 3d peaks indicates that the oxidation state of Gd remains +3 in CQD–DTPA–Gd [19,20,23]. All the above experimental results confirmed the successful synthesis of CQD–DTPA–Gd, and the surface functionalization did not change the crystal structure of CQDs.

Gd3+ chelation onto the surface of CQD–DTPA renders CQDs the MRI modality. The T 1 contrast capability of CQD–DTPA–Gd was investigated on a clinical 3.0T MRI instrument. Fig. 5 A showed the gray-scaled T 1-weighted MR images, and Fig. 5B presented the linear plot of relaxation rates versus equivalent Gd concentration. As illustrated in Fig. 5A, concentration-dependent positive contrast enhancement was observed for CQD–DTPA–Gd dispersions, validating their function of curtailing the longitudinal relaxation time of hydrogen protons. It should be noted that no such signal enhancement was observed for pure water (Fig. 5A) and bare CQDs (Fig. S5). As plotted in Fig. 5B, longitudinal relaxivity of CQD–DTPA–Gd was enhanced linearly along with the increase of equivalent Gd concentration. The longitudinal relaxivity of CQD–DTPA–Gd was calculated to be 56.72mM−1 s−1, which is significantly higher than commercially available MRI agent Gadovist (r 1 =4.34mM−1 s−1), recently reported Gd-doped CQDs (r 1 =5.88–11.5mM−1 s−1) (Table S2) [19–24] and Gd2O3 nanoparticles (r 1 =6.9mM−1 s−1) [39], indicating that CQD–DTPA–Gd synthesized in this study holds great potential as T 1-weighted MRI contrast agent in clinical application.

For Gd3+-based MRI contrast agent, a higher longitudinal relaxivity indicates a stronger signal enhancement and better imaging efficacy. Considering the extremely high toxicity of Gd3+, Gd-doped CQDs containing minimum amount Gd content while exhibiting a higher longitudinal relaxivity are still highly desirable. It has been reported that longitudinal relaxivity largely depends on direct interactions between Gd3+ and hydrogen protons, suggesting that only outer surface-chelated Gd3+ of Gd-doped CQDs could contribute to longitudinal relaxivity [22,28]. Therefore, the CQD–DTPA–Gd synthesized by current step-by-step strategy could perfectly fit the requirements. Correlated well with the XPS measurements, the Gd content in CQD–DTPA–Gd was quantitated to be 8.06±0.33% (w/w) by ICP-MS using HNO3 digestion, which was significantly lower than previous reports (Table S2) [19–24]. It is obvious that CQD–DTPA–Gd synthesized in this study only contains the outer surface-chelated Gd3+ which could be easily accessed by water molecules (Fig. 1). And the interactions between Gd3+ and hydrogen protons could be maximized by the high specific surface ratios of CQD–DTPA–Gd, thus possibly increasing the rotational correlation time and enhancing longitudinal relaxivity [23]. It is worth to mention that the relation between the Gd content on the longitudinal relaxivity deserve further investigation.

Gd3+ can induce serious cytotoxicity by disrupting critical Ca2+ signaling in cells [42–44]. It has been reported previously that IC50 and LD50 for free Gd3+ is ∼0.2mM and 0.4mmolkg−1, respectively [43–46]. The cytotoxicity of CQD–DTPA–Gd was evaluated using an MTT assay with 5.0μM cisplatin as positive control. As shown in Fig. S6A, the cell viability was over 90% even when the concentration of CQD–DTPA–Gd reaches 0.2mgmL−1 (equivalent Gd concentration is 1.02mM), confirming good biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity of CQD–DTPA–Gd towards HeLa cells. The strong Gd3+–DTPA chelation is expected to effectively block the leakage of Gd3+ into the surroundings [47]. To verify this hypothesis, CQD–DTPA–Gd was incubated in serum at 37°C for 24h. Gd3+ released into the surroundings was collected through dialysis and detected using Xylenol Orange as the indicator. The results in Fig. S6B confirmed the negligible leakage of Gd3+ from CQD–DTPA–Gd. To the contrary, in a control experiment where Gd3+ is conjugated onto the surface of as-prepared CQDs (without DTPA) through the interaction between its surface amino groups and Gd3+, results from Xylenol Orange assay revealed significant leakage of Gd3+ from CQD–Gd (Fig. S6C).

To demonstrate the feasibility of CQD–DTPA–Gd for fluorescent cell imaging, HeLa cells were incubated with 0.2mgmL−1 CQD–DTPA–Gd and visualized under confocal laser scanning microscope. Fig. 6 A–D show the fluorescence imaging microphotographs of CQD–DTPA–Gd treated HeLa cells under bright field, 405nm, 458nm and 514nm excitations. The bright-field image of Fig. 6A demonstrated the morphological integrity of cells after co-culturing with the CQD–DTPA–Gd, validating their biocompatibility. The fluorescence images of HeLa cells internalized with CQD–DTPA–Gd showed blue (Fig. 6B and E), green (Fig. 6C and F), red (Fig. 6D and G) three different colors under different excitation light source, correlating well with the characteristic wavelength-dependent emission of CQDs. CQD–DTPA–Gd were observed to localize in the whole cell area, weak emission signals from the nucleus and strong ones from the cytoplasm. It has been reported that the endosomolytic effect aroused by free amine groups can lead to cell membrane permeability [31,48–50]. Therefore, Kaiser tests have been performed to determine the free amines on the surface of CQD–DTPA–Gd. Results in Fig. S2 confirmed the presence of plenty of free amines groups (4.29%, w/w) on the surface of CQD–DTPA–Gd, suggesting that CQD–DTPA–Gd were internalized into the cells likely through endocytosis [48–50].

In summary, a new dual-modal bioimaging nanoprobe was facilely synthesized through surface conjugation of amine-terminated CQDs with cDTPAA and further Gd3+ chelation. The resultant nanoprobe CQD–DTPA–Gd exhibits excellent water solubility, strong fluorescence efficiency, extremely high relaxivity and negligible cytotoxicity. A series of clear and accurate characterization validated the successful preparation of CQD–DTPA–Gd. In vitro relaxivity characterization and in vivo imaging demonstrate that CQD–DTPA–Gd possesses both the high sensitivity of fluorescence imaging and the high spatial resolution of MRI, providing an opportunity for its practical applications in biological and clinical fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Scientific Foundation of China (31100723), Guangdong Innovative Research Team Program (2009010057), and Key Laboratory Funding Scheme Guangzhou Municipal Government (2060204) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: HRTEM image and size distribution histogram of as-prepared CQDs, Kaiser tests for free amines groups on the surface of as-prepared CQDs and CQD–DTPA–Gd, zoomed C 1s, N 1s, O 1s spectra, photoluminescence (PL) spectra of as-prepared CQDs and CQD–DTPA–Gd, T1-weighted MR images of as-prepared CQDs, viability of HeLa cells upon treatment of CQD–DTPA–Gd for 24h, Gd release from CQD–DTPA–Gd over time, and Gd leakage under different conditions. Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.05.100.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This document file contains Supplementary “Figs. S1–S6” and “Tables S1 and S2”.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Artificially engineered magnetic nanoparticles for ultra-sensitive molecular imaging
Superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles: from preparations to in vivo MRI applications
An X-ray computed tomography imaging agent based on long-circulating bismuth sulphide nanoparticles
In vivo cancer targeting and imaging with semiconductor quantum dots
A stimulus-responsive contrast agent for ultrasound molecular imaging
Strong two-photon-induced fluorescence from photostable, biocompatible nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for cellular and deep-tissue imaging
PET/MRI dual-modality tumor imaging using arginine-glycine-aspartic (RGD)-conjugated radiolabeled iron oxide nanoparticles
PEGylated WS(2) nanosheets as a multifunctional theranostic agent for in vivo dual-modal CT/photoacoustic imaging guided photothermal therapy
Detection of breast cancer microcalcifications using a dual-modality SPECT/NIR fluorescent probe
Self-assembled hybrid nanoparticles for cancer-specific multimodal imaging
Engineering of multifunctional nano-micelles for combined photothermal and photodynamic therapy under the guidance of multimodal imaging
Photouncaging nanoparticles for MRI and fluorescence imaging in vitro and in vivo
Multifunctional upconversion mesoporous silica nanostructures for dual modal imaging and in vivo drug delivery
Multifunctional upconversion nanoparticles for dual-modal imaging-guided stem cell therapy under remote magnetic control
Magnetic/upconversion fluorescent NaGdF4:Yb, Er nanoparticle-based dual-modal molecular probes for imaging tiny tumors in vivo
Activatable fluorescence/MRI bimodal platform for tumor cell imaging via MnO2 nanosheet-aptamer nanoprobe
New generation of multifunctional nanoparticles for cancer imaging and therapy
Multifunctional nanostructured materials for multimodal imaging, and simultaneous imaging and therapy
Gd(III)-doped carbon dots as a dual fluorescent-MRI probe
